title: Teriyaki Chicken

overview.description: Juicy chicken glazed in a homemade teriyaki sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake and sugar. Sweet, savoury and beautifully glossy, it's a Japanese classic that's surprisingly simple to make.

method: In a small bowl, combine the soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, ginger and garlic. Stir until the sugar has mostly dissolved.
Heat the vegetable oil in a large frying pan over a medium-high heat.
Add the chicken and cook for about 5–6 minutes on each side until golden and nearly cooked through.
Reduce the heat to medium and pour in the teriyaki sauce.
Cook for 4–5 minutes, turning the chicken regularly, until the sauce has reduced to a thick, glossy glaze that coats the chicken.
Remove from the heat and allow the chicken to rest for a couple of minutes.
Slice the chicken if serving whole, then spoon over any remaining glaze.
Scatter with the spring onions and sesame seeds before serving.

learning.did-you-know: The word 'teriyaki' comes from the Japanese words 'teri' (glaze or shine) and 'yaki' (to grill or cook). The glossy finish is the defining feature of the dish.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a traditional teriyaki glaze from scratch, how reduction thickens a sauce naturally, and how Japanese cooking often relies on a handful of high-quality ingredients.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A brilliant weeknight meal that introduces classic Japanese flavours while using just a handful of ingredients. Making the sauce from scratch is quick and produces a far better result than bottled alternatives.
chapman-tips: Don't let the sauce boil too fiercely once it begins to reduce or it may become too salty.
Chicken thighs stay much juicier than breast.
Use good-quality mirin if possible—it makes a noticeable difference to the finished glaze.
notes.batch-cooking: Freeze cooked chicken and sauce together for up to three months.
notes.leftovers: Excellent served cold in a lunchbox with rice or reheated gently in a frying pan.
